topic: Internal course: Critical Engagement with an Ethnographic Account
subject: sociology

items.q: Before evaluating an ethnographic account, what should a student identify?
items.correct: Its main argument about the society described.
items.why: Students skip locating the central claim, making their evaluation unfocused.

items.q: What does assessing the supporting evidence of an account require?
items.correct: Judging whether the fieldwork examples are relevant and sufficient.
items.misconception: Checking that the account has many footnotes.
items.why: Learners equate quantity of references with quality of evidence rather than assessing support.

items.q: Why must cultural context be considered when reading an account?
items.correct: Because practices only make sense within their own social setting.
items.misconception: Because context lets us rank cultures by progress.
items.random_wrong: Because context reveals the author's salary.
items.why: Students misuse context to judge cultures instead of to interpret practices fairly.

items.q: The first step in critically engaging with an ethnographic account is to:
items.why: It's Identify its main argument. “Translate it into another language” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Assessing the 'supporting evidence' of an account involves judging whether it is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Relevant and sufficient. “Neatly typed” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Considering 'cultural context' helps a reader to:
items.correct: Interpret practices within their own setting
items.why: It's Interpret practices within their own setting. “Rank cultures by progress” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A balanced evaluation of an ethnographic account identifies its:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Strengths and weaknesses. “Strengths only” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
